name: Al-Khafid

names.en: The Abaser, The One who brings low
The Exalter, The One who raises

title: Al-Khafid: The Abaser (with Ar-Rafi, The Exalter)
meta_description: Al-Khafid, the One who brings low, built from the Qur'an and classical tafsir. The Day that lowers some and raises others, the arrogant who boasted brought down, the plot that perishes. Grounded in khafidatun rafi'a (56:3) and the Names literature. Always taught with Ar-Rafi. Qur'an verified.
keywords: Al-Khafid, Al-Khaafid, Ar-Rafi, The Abaser, The One who brings low, The Exalter, names of Allah, 99 names, Asma ul Husna, khafidatun rafia, Surah Al-Waqia 56:3, Allah lowers the arrogant, izzah belongs to Allah, Buruja
source_note: Grounded in the Qur'an (Sahih International, verified via quran.ai) and classical tafsir (Ibn Kathir, Tafsir as-Sa'di, al-Tabari, al-Qurtubi, and Ibn 'Ashur's al-Tahrir wa-l-Tanwir), in the voice of Buruja. Note on the name-form: the definite name Al-Khafid does not appear in the Qur'an; it is grounded in the verbal form khafidatun (56:3) and in the Names literature, as explained in the opening section. Al-Qurtubi states plainly that the true Khafid and Rafi is Allah alone.
lede: We live in a world that looks upside down. The arrogant are on top. The ones who lie loudest, who push to the front, who step on whoever is beneath them, seem to rise and rise, while the quiet and the wronged and the honest stay low. And a question forms in you that you may be ashamed to say out loud: does anyone ever set this right? Is the order of things really the way it looks from down here?

This name answers that, and it does not answer it gently. Al-Khafid, the One who brings low, who abases, who pulls the high down to where they belong. The tradition almost never lets you hold this name alone, though. It hands it to you joined to its companion, Ar-Rafi, the Exalter, the One who lifts the lowly up. He lowers and He raises, and these are not two moods of God at war with each other. They are one perfectly just Hand, and the Day is coming when that Hand will turn the whole upside-down world the right way up, in front of everyone.

sections.h2: A name the Qur'an gives as the Day's own work

ayah.text: It will bring down [some] and raise up [others].

paras: Let us be honest with you from the first line, because this name deserves it. Unlike many of the names in this series, you will not find the word Al-Khafid sitting in the Qur'an in its definite form, the way you find As-Sami or Al-Aziz. What the Qur'an gives you instead is the living word in action. In Surah Al-Waqi'a, describing the Day of Resurrection when it finally falls, Allah says of that Day: khafidatun rafi'a, it brings down and it raises up. Khafidah, lowering. Rafi'ah, raising. The two are spoken in a single breath, two words side by side, and the whole reversal of creation is folded into them.

The name Al-Khafid, then, is how the scholars of the tradition gathered that word into a name, and they did not do it lightly. Al-Qurtubi, commenting on this very verse, says it directly: khafd and rafa', lowering and raising, the Arabs use these words for place and for rank, for honor and for humiliation. And then he settles the matter of the name itself in one sentence worth holding onto: wa al-Khafid wa al-Rafi 'ala al-haqiqa innama huwa Allah wahdah, and the One who truly lowers and the One who truly raises is Allah alone, who raised His friends to the highest of degrees and lowered His enemies to the lowest of depths. The Day does the lowering in the verse, but al-Qurtubi reminds you Who is really behind the Day.

So when we call Allah Al-Khafid, we are not pinning a label on Him that He did not give. We are taking the word He used of the great Day He is bringing, khafidah, and holding it the way the tradition has always held it: as a true name of God, grounded in His own word, even though the definite form Al-Khafid is the tradition's way of carrying it rather than a single Qur'anic title. We wanted you to know exactly where this name stands before we ask you to lean on it. And notice already, in the verse itself, that lowering never comes alone. In the same breath as khafidah is rafi'ah. He does not only bring down. He raises, in the very same act.

The Qur'an leaves the Day's lowering and raising as two bare words, khafidatun rafi'a, and the early scholars rushed to fill in who it is that gets lowered and who gets lifted. Read their answers together and a clear picture forms. Ibn Kathir sums it up: that Day lowers peoples down to the lowest of the low, to the Hellfire, even though they were mighty and honored in this world, wa in kanu fi al-dunya a'izza, and it raises others up to the highest heights, to lasting bliss, even though they were lowly in this world, wa in kanu fi al-dunya wuda'a. Sit on that reversal. The exact people the world placed at the top are at the bottom, and the ones the world stepped over are raised highest.

The reports Ibn Kathir, al-Tabari, and al-Qurtubi carry name the lowered ones precisely, and they are exactly who you suspected. As-Suddi: khafadat al-mutakabbirin wa rafa'at al-mutawadi'in, it lowered the arrogant and raised the humble. 'Uthman ibn Saraqa, a cousin of Umar ibn al-Khattab: the Hour lowered the enemies of Allah into the Fire and raised the friends of Allah into the Garden. Muhammad ibn Ka'b: it lowers men who in this world were elevated, and raises men who in this world were brought down. Ibn 'Ata adds a beautiful pair: it lowered some bi-l-'adl, by justice, and raised others bi-l-fadl, by grace. Nothing arbitrary is happening. The arrogant are met with justice. The lowly are met with grace.

Ibn 'Ashur, the great rhetorician, reads the verse the same way and names the lowered ones without flinching: al-jababira wa al-mufsidin, the tyrants and the corruptors, who were in this world in elevation and lordship, are brought down, while the righteous, whom most people did not even bother to notice, are raised. He also points out something the Arabic is doing on purpose. Khafidah and rafi'ah set two opposites against each other in one phrase, a figure the rhetoricians call tibaq, antithesis, both extremes affirmed of the single Day. The verse is built to make you feel the whole world flipping in two words.

ayah.arabic: يَقُولُونَ لَئِن رَّجَعْنَا إِلَى الْمَدِينَةِ لَيُخْرِجَنَّ الْأَعَزُّ مِنْهَا الْأَذَلَّ ۚ وَلِلَّهِ الْعِزَّةُ وَلِرَسُولِهِ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَلَٰكِنَّ الْمُنَافِقِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ
ayah.text: They say, "If we return to al-Madinah, the more honored [for power] will surely expel therefrom the more humble." And to Allah belongs [all] honor, and to His Messenger, and to the believers, but the hypocrites do not know.

paras: You do not have to wait for the Last Day to see Al-Khafid at work. The Qur'an shows you the lowering of the arrogant happening inside history, and there is no clearer scene than this one. Al-Sa'di tells the story: it was on a military expedition, and some friction had broken out between a few of the Muhajirun and the Ansar, and in that moment the hypocrisy of the hypocrites surfaced and they said what was really in their hearts. Their leader, Abdullah ibn Ubayy, sneered that when they got back to Madina, the mightier party, meaning himself and his faction, would drive out the lowlier party, meaning the Prophet and the believers, by his reckoning.

He had the whole thing exactly backwards, and Allah turned it over in the next breath of the verse. Al-Sa'di spells it out: the matter is the reverse of what this hypocrite said, which is why Allah declared, and to Allah belongs all honor, and to His Messenger, and to the believers. They are the truly honored ones, al-a'izza, and the hypocrites and their fellow disbelievers are the truly debased ones, al-adhilla. The man who called himself al-a'azz, the mightier, was in reality the lower. The believers he sneered at as al-adhall, the lowlier, were in reality the higher. Al-Khafid had quietly swapped the labels he was so sure of.

And then al-Sa'di names the saddest part: wa lakinna al-munafiqin la ya'lamun, but the hypocrites do not know it. They went on believing they were the mighty ones, deceived by the falsehood they stood on, ightiraran bi-ma hum 'alayhi min al-batil. That is the quiet horror inside being brought low by Allah. It is not only that the arrogant are lowered. It is that they are often the last to know, still feeling tall while the ground has already opened beneath them. We might reflect that this is the most frightening way to fall: certain you are rising, the whole time you are sinking.

sections.h2: Real honor is in one Hand only

ayah.arabic: مَن كَانَ يُرِيدُ الْعِزَّةَ فَلِلَّهِ الْعِزَّةُ جَمِيعًا ۚ إِلَيْهِ يَصْعَدُ الْكَلِمُ الطَّيِّبُ وَالْعَمَلُ الصَّالِحُ يَرْفَعُهُ ۚ وَالَّذِينَ يَمْكُرُونَ السَّيِّئَاتِ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ ۖ وَمَكْرُ أُولَٰئِكَ هُوَ يَبُورُ
ayah.text: Whoever desires honor [through power] - then to Allah belongs all honor. To Him ascends good speech, and righteous work raises it. But they who plot evil deeds will have a severe punishment, and the plotting of those - it will perish.

paras: If the arrogant are the ones brought low, where is a person supposed to go to actually rise? This verse answers it, and the answer reroutes your whole sense of ambition. Whoever wants 'izzah, real honor, weight, standing, then know that all of it belongs to Allah. Al-Sa'di puts the lesson bluntly: O you who want honor, seek it from the One in whose Hand it lies, for honor is in Allah's Hand and is not attained except by His obedience. You cannot grab it by climbing over people. It is not on the shelf you are reaching for. It is given, by Him, on His terms.

And the verse shows you the only thing that genuinely rises. To Him ascends the good word, and the righteous deed lifts it. Al-Sa'di explains that the good word, every recitation and tasbih and praise and good clean speech, is raised up to Allah and presented to Him, and Allah praises its author before the highest assembly. The righteous deed is what carries it up. This is the true rafa', the real raising, and it is available to the lowest, quietest, most overlooked believer on earth. You raise yourself not by being seen by people but by sending good words and good deeds up to the One who raises.

Now watch what happens to the schemer in the same verse. Those who plot evil have a severe punishment, and their plot, huwa yabur, it perishes. Al-Sa'di draws the contrast exactly along the lines of our two names: as for the evil deeds, they are the reverse, their doer wants to rise by them, and he plots and schemes, and it all comes back on him, and he gains nothing but more humiliation and more sinking, la yazdadu illa ihanatan wa nuzula. Read that slowly. The plotting that was meant to lift him is the very thing that lowers him. He engineered his own descent. Al-Khafid does not always need to push the arrogant down. Often He simply lets their schemes run, and the schemes do the lowering for Him.

sections.h2: Lifted high, then dropped to the lowest

ayah.arabic: لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنسَانَ فِي أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ
ayah.text: We have certainly created man in the best of stature;
Then We return him to the lowest of the low,

ayah.arabic: إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ فَلَهُمْ أَجْرٌ غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ
ayah.text: Except for those who believe and do righteous deeds, for they will have a reward uninterrupted.

paras: There is a verse that puts the whole drama of khafd and rafa' onto every single human being, and it should make you check which way you are facing. Allah says He created the human being fi ahsani taqwim, in the most beautiful, most upright stature, lifted in form and faculty above the rest of creation. That is where every one of us begins: raised, honored, given a height. And then comes the turn: thumma radadnahu asfala safilin, then We returned him to the lowest of the low. The same phrase, asfal safilin, that Ibn Kathir used for where the arrogant land on the Last Day.

Ibn Kathir, commenting here, says asfal safilin means to the Fire, after that beauty and that fine stature, if he does not obey Allah and follow the messengers. This is the most sobering shape this name takes. The lowering is not only something that happens to distant tyrants. Every human is handed a height at the start, and what we do with it decides whether we keep rising or are dropped to the bottom. The gift of being lifted is not the same as the safety of staying lifted.

But do not miss the exception, because it is the whole hope of the surah: illa alladhina amanu wa 'amilu al-salihat, except those who believe and do righteous deeds, for them is a reward without end. Here the two names split the human race in two. Faith and good action are what keep you in the company of Ar-Rafi, raised, while the descent to asfal safilin is what waits for the one who wastes the height he was given. The fence between being raised forever and being lowered forever is exactly the iman and the 'amal salih the verse names. You are not passive in this. You are choosing, every day, which of His two hands you are walking into.

sections.h2: The companion name: the One who raises

ayah.arabic: رَفِيعُ الدَّرَجَاتِ ذُو الْعَرْشِ يُلْقِي الرُّوحَ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَاءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ لِيُنذِرَ يَوْمَ التَّلَاقِ
ayah.text: [He is] the Exalted above [all] degrees, Owner of the Throne; He places the inspiration of His command [i.e., revelation] upon whom He wills of His servants to warn of the Day of Meeting.

paras: We have kept Ar-Rafi walking beside Al-Khafid this whole way, because the tradition will not separate them, and now it is time to look straight at the raising. Al-Khafid is never a God who only takes people down. The same Day that lowers the arrogant raises the friends of Allah to lasting bliss, in the very same verse. And the Qur'an names the raising as one of His attributes: Rafi' al-darajat, the One who is high above all degrees, and the One who raises others through them. Al-Sa'di explains the phrase as al-Ali al-A'la, the High, the Most High, whose own rank is raised infinitely above His creation, and who lifts the ranks of His servants and draws them near and places them above His other creatures.

And He tells you exactly how He raises. Al-Sa'di, on this same verse, says the thing that lifts a servant's degrees is the pure, purifying deed, which is sincerity, ikhlas, the deed done for His face alone, alladhi yarfa'u darajat ashabihi wa yuqarribuhum ilayhi. There is the answer to every lowly believer's quiet ache to matter. You rise not by being loud, not by climbing over anyone, but by sincerity, which the most invisible person on earth can have in full. The Hand that lowers the arrogant is the very same Hand reaching down to lift the sincere.

Hold the two names together and they steady you completely. If you only had Al-Khafid, you might live braced and afraid, as if God's nature were to bring everyone down. If you only had Ar-Rafi, you might forget that arrogance is ever answered at all. The pair tells the truth: the same wise, just Hand lowers the one who made himself high on falsehood and raises the one who made himself low before his Lord. He is not sometimes cruel and sometimes kind. He is always just, and justice looks like lowering in one direction and raising in the other, at the very same moment.

sections.h2: How to live under these two names

ayah.arabic: وَإِذَا قِيلَ انشُزُوا فَانشُزُوا يَرْفَعِ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنكُمْ وَالَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ دَرَجَاتٍ ۚ وَاللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ
ayah.text: And when you are told, "Arise," then arise; Allah will raise those who have believed among you and those who were given knowledge, by degrees. And Allah is Aware of what you do.

paras: A name of Allah is never only information. Al-Khafid and Ar-Rafi are meant to reshape you, and they do it in at least three ways.

First, they kill arrogance at the root. Once you truly believe that the One who brings low is watching, you cannot keep making yourself big at the expense of others, because you have seen what He does to exactly that. The mutakabbirun, as-Suddi said, are the ones He lowers. Abdullah ibn Ubayy called himself the mightier and was named the lowest. So you stop reaching for height by stepping on people. You remember that whoever wants honor must seek it from the only One who owns it, and that the surest way to be brought down is to lift yourself up on the heads of others.

Second, they comfort you when you are the one being stepped on. If you are low right now, overlooked, wronged, watching lesser people pass you, this name is a quiet promise that the arrangement is not final and not even real. The Day is khafidatun rafi'a. Al-Khafid sees the one who made himself tall over you, and Ar-Rafi sees you. Your job is not to claw your way up by their methods. Your job is to send good words and good deeds upward, because the good word ascends and the righteous deed raises it, and to trust the Hand that raised the wuda'a, the lowly ones, to the highest heights.

Third, they tell you how to actually rise. Look at how the Qur'an describes it: arise when you are called to make room and serve, and Allah will raise those who believe and those given knowledge by degrees. Al-Sa'di reads it simply: Allah raises the people of knowledge and faith by degrees, according to what He has singled them out with of knowledge and faith. That is the whole ladder. Not status, not noise, not scheming. Iman, 'ilm, sincere deeds quietly sent up. You lower yourself before Him, and He raises you. That is the only climb that ends anywhere but asfal safilin.

sections.h2: The Day the world turns right way up
paras: Step back and feel the whole shape of it. Al-Khafid is the lowering in a universe that desperately needs lowering, and Ar-Rafi is the raising that always answers it, and both are one perfectly just Hand. The Qur'an gave the two as a single breath, khafidatun rafi'a, and that is the truth of the Day that is coming and of every smaller reckoning along the way: nothing high on falsehood stays high, and nothing low in sincerity stays low.

This is the answer to the upside-down world you started with. Right now the arrogant look like they are winning and the honest look like they are losing, and it can shake you. But you are reading the scene at halftime. Al-Khafid has not finished. The tyrants in elevation, the corruptors in lordship, the schemer certain he is rising, the man who sneered that he was the mightier, are all of them being lowered, some already and not even knowing it, all of them by the Last Day. And the overlooked believer, the wronged one, the quiet servant nobody made room for, is being raised by degrees toward the highest of the high. The whole world is being turned the right way up, and on that Day everyone will see it at once.

So do not envy the high and do not despair at being low. Ask only to be on the right side of these two names. Make yourself small before Allah so that you are never made small by Him. Seek your honor from the only Hand that owns it. And when you watch falsehood tower over truth, remember the two words the Qur'an folded the whole Day into, and let them hold you: khafidatun rafi'a. He brings down, and He raises up, and He is not finished yet.

_Note: reading the Day's lowering and raising in 56:3 onto the present injustices of our own world, and onto the personal experience of being overlooked or wronged, is contemplative reflection drawn from the mufassirun's glosses (the arrogant lowered and the humble raised in as-Suddi, the tyrants and corruptors brought down in Ibn 'Ashur, honor belonging to Allah in al-Sa'di), offered as tadabbur and not as a formal scholarly ruling or consensus._

dua.arabic: اللَّهُمَّ يَا خَافِضُ يَا رَافِعُ، ارْفَعْنِي بِطَاعَتِكَ وَلَا تَخْفِضْنِي بِمَعْصِيَتِكَ
dua.translit: Allahumma ya Khafidu ya Rafi'u, irfa'ni bi-ta'atika wa la takhfidni bi-ma'siyatika
dua.en: O Allah, O Abaser, O Exalter, raise me by Your obedience and do not bring me low by disobedience to You.

lessons.bold: Never make yourself big over people.
lessons.text: As-Suddi said the Day lowers the arrogant (al-mutakabbirin) and raises the humble. Abdullah ibn Ubayy called himself the mightier and Allah named him the lowest. The surest way to be brought down is to lift yourself on the heads of others.

lessons.bold: Seek honor only from the One who owns it.
lessons.text: Al-Sa'di teaches that izzah is in Allah's Hand and is reached only through His obedience (35:10). You cannot grab honor by climbing over anyone. It is given by Ar-Rafi, on His terms, to whoever turns to Him.

The good word ascends to Allah and the righteous deed raises it (35:10), and He raises the people of faith and knowledge by degrees (58:11). The most overlooked believer can rise this way. Send good words and deeds up, and leave the raising to Him.

lessons.bold: Take heart when you are the one stepped on.
lessons.text: The Day is khafidatun rafi'a (56:3): it lowers the mighty who were a'izza in the world and raises the lowly who were wuda'a, as Ibn Kathir explains. The arrangement you see now is halftime, not the final score. Ar-Rafi sees you.

lessons.bold: Fear rising on falsehood most of all.
lessons.text: The hypocrites 'do not know' they are the debased ones (63:8), and the schemer's own plot perishes and only sinks him further (35:10). The most dangerous fall is the one you take while certain you are climbing. Build your height on truth, or it is no height at all.

faq.q: What does the name Al-Khafid mean?
faq.a: Al-Khafid (الخافض) means The Abaser or The One who brings low, from the root kh-f-d, whose basic sense is to lower, bring down, or humble. Applied to Allah it means the One who lowers the arrogant, the tyrants, and the people of falsehood, by His perfect justice, casting down whoever raised himself up against the truth. Commenting on Surah Al-Waqi'a 56:3, Ibn Kathir explains that the Day brings some down to the lowest of the low even though they were mighty in the world, and as-Suddi glosses it as lowering the arrogant and raising the humble. It is classically taught with its companion Ar-Rafi, the Exalter, never alone.

faq.q: Is Al-Khafid actually in the Qur'an?
faq.a: Not in the definite name-form (الْخَافِض). What the Qur'an gives is the verbal form: in Surah Al-Waqi'a 56:3, the Day of Resurrection is described as khafidatun rafi'a, 'it brings down [some] and raises up [others].' The root kh-f-d appears only four times in the Qur'an, and the other three are the gentle command to 'lower the wing' of humility and mercy (17:24, 15:88, 26:215), so the sense of abasing rests on 56:3 together with the Names literature. The name itself is established in the classical tradition: al-Qurtubi, commenting on 56:3, states plainly that 'the One who truly lowers and the One who truly raises is Allah alone' (al-Khafid wa al-Rafi 'ala al-haqiqa innama huwa Allah wahdah). So the name rests on the word Allah used of the Day He is bringing, with the definite form carried by the tradition.

faq.q: Why is Al-Khafid always paired with Ar-Rafi?
faq.a: Because the Qur'an pairs them. The verse that grounds both, 56:3, speaks them in a single phrase: khafidatun rafi'a, lowering and raising, in one breath. Ibn 'Ashur notes this is a deliberate antithesis (tibaq), both opposites affirmed of the one Day. Held alone, Al-Khafid could be misread as a God whose nature is only to bring people down, and Ar-Rafi as if arrogance is never answered. Together they teach that the same just Hand lowers the one who made himself high on falsehood and raises the one who humbled himself before his Lord, at the very same moment. The lowering and the raising are two motions of one justice, not two competing natures.

faq.q: If Allah is Al-Khafid, why do arrogant people seem to keep winning?
faq.a: The Qur'an answers that you are reading the scene before it is finished. In Surah Al-Munafiqun 63:8, the hypocrite Abdullah ibn Ubayy boasted that 'the more honored will expel the more humble,' and Allah immediately overturned it: honor belongs to Allah, His Messenger, and the believers. Al-Sa'di notes the chilling detail that 'the hypocrites do not know' they are the debased ones; they feel mighty while already being lowered. And in 35:10, the schemer's own plot 'perishes' and, as al-Sa'di says, only sinks him further. So the apparent victory of the arrogant is temporary and often an illusion even now, and on the Day that is khafidatun rafi'a (56:3) it is completely reversed in front of everyone. (Applying this to a specific injustice you are watching is reflection on the tafsir, not a ruling.)

closing_heading: Why this name stays with us
closing: We started with an upside-down world, where the arrogant rise and the honest stay low, and this name meets us there without pretending the view does not sting. Al-Khafid is the One who brings low, and the Qur'an gives Him to us in the Day's own work, khafidatun rafi'a, spoken in the same breath as the raising, so we never hold the lowering without the lifting. He brings down the mighty who were a'izza in the world and raises the lowly who were wuda'a, as Ibn Kathir reads it. He overturned the boast of the hypocrite who called himself the mightier and was named the lowest. He lets the schemer's plot perish in his own hands. And the same just Hand that lowers the arrogant is reaching down to raise the sincere by their faith and their quiet deeds. To know Al-Khafid and Ar-Rafi is to stop envying the high and stop despairing at the low, and to ask only to be on the right side of these two names when the world finally turns the right way up.

O Allah, Al-Khafid and Ar-Rafi, the One who brings low and the One who raises up, we have watched falsehood tower over truth and it has shaken us. Help us never to build our height on anything but You. Make us small before You so that we are never made small by You. Raise us by Your obedience, by faith and sincere deeds quietly sent up to You, and do not lower us by our own arrogance or our own schemes. And when we are wronged and overlooked, let us trust that the Day is coming when You will set every high and low thing in its true place. Allahumma ya Khafidu ya Rafi'u, irfa'ni bi-ta'atika wa la takhfidni bi-ma'siyatika.

slides:
Al-Khafid.

The One who brings low. The Abaser.

We live in a world that looks upside down: the arrogant on top, the honest kept low. And you wonder if anyone ever sets it right.

This name does not answer gently.

First, an honest note.

Al-Khafid is not in the Qur'an as a definite name (الْخَافِض). What the Qur'an gives is the Day's own work:

'**khafidatun rafi'a**', it brings down [some] and raises up [others]. (56:3)

The name is how the tradition gathered that word.

And the Qur'an will not let you hold it alone.

In one breath: **khafidah** AND **rafi'ah.** Lowering AND raising.

Al-Qurtubi: 'the One who truly lowers and truly raises is Allah alone' (al-Khafid wa al-Rafi).

The Hand that drops the arrogant lifts the lowly.

Who exactly gets brought low?

as-Suddi: **the arrogant** (al-mutakabbirin), and it raised the humble.

Ibn Kathir: those mighty in the world (a'izza), down to the lowest, and the lowly (wuda'a) raised highest.

Ibn 'Ashur: the tyrants and the corruptors.

Watch it happen in history.

Abdullah ibn Ubayy sneered: 'the mightier will expel the lowlier' (63:8), meaning HE was mighty and the Prophet ﷺ lowly.

Allah flipped it: 'honor belongs to Allah, His Messenger, and the believers.'

He called himself highest. He was named lowest.

And the scariest part:

'**but the hypocrites do not know**' (63:8).

The arrogant are often the last to feel the fall. Certain they are rising, the whole time they are sinking.

The most dangerous fall is the one you take while sure you are climbing.

So how do you actually rise?

Not by climbing over people. 'Whoever wants honor, all honor belongs to Allah.' (35:10)

'The good word ascends to Him, and the righteous deed raises it.'

The most overlooked believer can rise this way. 🤍

Meet the companion name: **Ar-Rafi**, the Exalter.

'Rafi' al-darajat', high above all degrees. (40:15)

al-Sa'di: what lifts a servant's rank is sincerity (ikhlas), the deed for His face alone.

The Hand that lowers the arrogant reaches down to raise the sincere.

Ya Khafid, ya Rafi.

The arrogant look like they're winning. But you're reading the scene at halftime.

Raise me by Your obedience. Do not lower me by my own arrogance.

The world is being turned right way up, and the Day will show it. 🤍
social.caption:
We live in a world that looks upside down. The arrogant rise, the honest stay low, and you wonder if anyone ever sets it right.

Al-Khafid, the One who brings low, meets you there. One honest note first: this name does not appear in the Qur'an in its definite form. What the Qur'an gives is the Day's own work, 'khafidatun rafi'a,' it brings down some and raises up others (56:3). The name is how the classical tradition gathered that word, and al-Qurtubi states plainly that 'the One who truly lowers and truly raises is Allah alone.'

Notice the Qur'an speaks the lowering and the raising in one breath. The tradition always teaches Al-Khafid beside Ar-Rafi, the Exalter, because the same just Hand drops the arrogant and lifts the lowly. And the scholars name exactly who is brought low: as-Suddi says the arrogant (al-mutakabbirin), Ibn 'Ashur says the tyrants and the corruptors, Ibn Kathir says those who were mighty in the world, cast down even as the lowly are raised highest.

You can watch it happen in history. The hypocrite Abdullah ibn Ubayy sneered that 'the mightier will expel the lowlier' (63:8), certain he was the mighty one and the Prophet ﷺ the lowly. Allah overturned it in the next breath: honor belongs to Allah, His Messenger, and the believers. He called himself highest and was named lowest, and 'the hypocrites do not know' it, still feeling tall while sinking.

So how do you rise? Not by climbing over anyone. 'Whoever wants honor, all honor belongs to Allah' (35:10). The good word ascends to Him and the righteous deed raises it, and He raises the people of faith and knowledge by degrees (58:11). The most overlooked believer can rise this way.

The arrogant look like they are winning, but you are reading the scene at halftime. The world is being turned the right way up.

What would change if you truly believed the high and low you see now are not final? 🤍

Save this, and read the full reflection: buruja.com/names-of-allah/al-khafid

Grounded in the Qur'an (Sahih International, via quran.ai) and classical tafsir (Ibn Kathir, al-Sa'di, al-Tabari, al-Qurtubi, Ibn 'Ashur).

#AlKhafid #ArRafi #NamesOfAllah #AsmaUlHusna #99Names #Justice #Humility #Dua #IslamicReminders #Quran #Buruja #Islam

email.subject: The boast that history answered
email.alt_subject: The name of Allah for an upside-down world
email.preview: Al-Khafid, the One who brings low. The arrogant who look like they are winning are being read at halftime, and the Day is khafidatun rafi'a.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

We live in a world that looks upside down. The arrogant are on top. The ones who lie loudest, who push to the front, who step on whoever is beneath them, seem to rise and rise, while the quiet and the wronged and the honest stay low. And a question forms that we are sometimes ashamed to say out loud: does anyone ever set this right?

One of the names of Allah answers that, and it does not answer gently: Al-Khafid, the One who brings low, the Abaser.

Let us be honest with you from the start, because this name deserves it. Unlike many of the names we write about, Al-Khafid does not appear in the Qur'an in its definite form. What the Qur'an gives instead is the living word in action. Describing the Day of Resurrection when it finally falls, Allah says of that Day: 'khafidatun rafi'a,' it brings down some and raises up others (Al-Waqi'a 56:3). The name is how the scholars of the tradition gathered that word, and they did not do it lightly. Al-Qurtubi, commenting on this very verse, settles it in one sentence: 'the One who truly lowers and the One who truly raises is Allah alone,' who raised His friends to the highest of degrees and lowered His enemies to the lowest of depths.

Notice what the verse refuses to do. It will not let you hear the lowering on its own. In the same breath comes the raising. The tradition has carried them as a pair ever since, Al-Khafid beside Ar-Rafi, the Exalter, because the same just Hand that brings the arrogant down is the very Hand that lifts the lowly up. And the scholars name exactly who is lowered: as-Suddi says the arrogant, Ibn 'Ashur says the tyrants and the corruptors who were in this world in elevation and lordship, and Ibn Kathir says those who were mighty in the world are cast to the lowest of the low, even as the lowly are raised highest.

You can watch it happen inside history. On a military expedition, the hypocrite Abdullah ibn Ubayy sneered that when they returned to Madina 'the mightier will expel the lowlier,' meaning, al-Sa'di explains, that he and his faction were the mighty and the Prophet, peace and blessings be upon him, and the believers were the lowly. Allah overturned it in the next breath: 'and to Allah belongs all honor, and to His Messenger, and to the believers' (63:8). The man who called himself the mightier was named the lowest. And al-Sa'di notes the saddest detail: 'but the hypocrites do not know' it. They went on feeling mighty while the ground had already opened beneath them. That is the most frightening way to fall, certain you are rising the whole time you are sinking.

So how does a person actually rise? Not by climbing over anyone. 'Whoever wants honor, then to Allah belongs all honor' (35:10), and, al-Sa'di says, it is reached only through His obedience. The verse shows the one thing that genuinely ascends: 'to Him ascends the good word, and the righteous deed raises it.' That is the real raising, and it is available to the lowest, quietest, most overlooked believer alive. Meanwhile the schemer's own plot, the verse says, 'perishes,' and al-Sa'di notes it only sinks him further. Al-Khafid often does not need to push the arrogant down. He lets their schemes run, and the schemes do the lowering.

Here, then, is the comfort of this name. If the arrogant around you look like they are winning, you are reading the scene at halftime. The Day is khafidatun rafi'a, and it is not finished. So make yourself small before Allah, so that you are never made small by Him. Seek your honor from the only Hand that owns it. And when falsehood towers over truth, hold onto the two words the Qur'an folded the whole Day into.

Ya Khafid, ya Rafi, raise us by Your obedience, and do not lower us by our own arrogance.

If you would like the full reflection, we have written it here:
